coredata.dc:title: Tailored sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dot interfacial layer on copper foil for highly stable and ultrafast lithium-ion capacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Sung, Ki-Wook
Ko, Keun-Young
Ahn, Hyo-Jin
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) are promising energy storage devices offering the advantages of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and supercapacitors (SCs). However, the interfacial structure between the electrode and current collector significantly affects their cycling performance and stability during ultrafast cycling. Therefore, we propose a tailored sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dot (SNCQD) interfacial layer on a copper foil (SNCQD/Cu) using an ultrasonic spray-coating system. A half-cell LIC electrode, fabricated with the SNCQD/Cu as the current collector, exhibited a superior specific capacity of 80.8 mAh/g and capacity retention of 90.1 % after 500 cycles at an ultrafast current density of 3000 mA/g. Furthermore, the electrode demonstrated excellent ultrafast cycling stability of 84.7 % at a capacity rate of 5C in full-cell LICs. These results are attributed to the synergistic effect of the SNCQD interfacial layer, which prevents oxidation and corrosion, enhances interfacial adhesion, and increases electrical conductivity. Therefore, the introduction of an SNCQD interfacial layer can help achieve highly stable, ultrafast LICs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion capacitors
Anode
Current collector
Interfacial engineering
Carbon quantum dot

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of fabrication of (a) S, N co-doped carbon quantum dot and (b) SNCQD interfacial layer coated Cu foil.
(a) Low- and (b) high-resolution TEM images, (c) FFT result of SNCQDs, and (d) UV–vis absorbance spectrum of SNCQD dispersion.
(a)–(c) FESEM images of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu respectively, and (d) cross-sectional TEM images of SNCQD/Cu after FIB milling.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, and (c) S 2p of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu, respectively.
(a) Nyquist plots with equivalent circuit diagram and (b) bode plots of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes.
(a) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves, (b) cycling stability, (c) rate performance, (d) ultrafast cycling performance of LIC half-cell of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at C-rate of (a) 0.1C and (b) 5C, (C) rate performance at C-rate of 0.1C, 0.5C, 1C, 3C, and 5C, and (d) capacity retention and (e) Coulombic efficiency at ultrafast C-rate of 5C for 3000 cycles of LIC full-cell of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes.
(a)–(c) Low- and (d)–(f) high-resolution cross-sectional FESEM images of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu after ultrafast cycling tests.

head.ce:title: Tailored sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dot interfacial layer on copper foil for highly stable and ultrafast lithium-ion capacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Seoul National University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: K.-W. Sung and K.-Y. Ko contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) are promising energy storage devices offering the advantages of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and supercapacitors (SCs). However, the interfacial structure between the electrode and current collector significantly affects their cycling performance and stability during ultrafast cycling. Therefore, we propose a tailored sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dot (SNCQD) interfacial layer on a copper foil (SNCQD/Cu) using an ultrasonic spray-coating system. A half-cell LIC electrode, fabricated with the SNCQD/Cu as the current collector, exhibited a superior specific capacity of 80.8 mAh/g and capacity retention of 90.1 % after 500 cycles at an ultrafast current density of 3000 mA/g. Furthermore, the electrode demonstrated excellent ultrafast cycling stability of 84.7 % at a capacity rate of 5C in full-cell LICs. These results are attributed to the synergistic effect of the SNCQD interfacial layer, which prevents oxidation and corrosion, enhances interfacial adhesion, and increases electrical conductivity. Therefore, the introduction of an SNCQD interfacial layer can help achieve highly stable, ultrafast LICs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing concerns about global warming, primarily linked to the use of fossil fuels, have driven the demand for alternative energy sources lately [1,2]. Consequently, there has been a surge in interest in renewable energy and various energy storage systems (ESSs) [3–5]. Among the various ESSs, lithium (Li)-ion capacitors (LICs) have gained attention because of their higher power density and cycling stability than those of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) and higher energy density than those of supercapacitors (SCs) [6–8]. LICs are hybrid-type SCs comprising LIB-type anodes and SC-type cathodes, which undergo two processes during charging and discharging: (a) Li intercalation–deintercalation at the graphite anode and (b) formation–depletion of the electrical double layer at the activated carbon (C)-based cathode [7,9,10]. However, the sluggish kinetics of Li diffusion can lead to a low intercalation–deintercalation rate, making the ultrafast cycling capability of LICs heavily dependent on the anode. Therefore, it is crucial to improve the LIC anodes to achieve ultrafast charging and discharging capabilities.

Although graphite-based anode materials have been widely used commercially, their performance at high current densities is limited because of the low diffusion rate during charging [7,8]. Soft C has recently emerged as a potential active material for the anode of ultrafast LICs, having demonstrated superior cycling performance at high current densities because of its fast Li-ion diffusion rate, low self-discharge and cyclability [11]. Several studies have focused on increasing the specific capacity and cycling stability of soft C. Yan et al. reported the morning glory-like porous structure of soft C [12]; Sun et al. reported a SiOx–soft C composite [13]; and Lim et al. have reported the conductive polymer and soft C composite [14]. Moreover, other than the use of active materials, multiple studies have focused on enhancing the ultrafast cycling capability of anodes using binders in electrodes [15], additives in electrolytes [16,17], and pre-lithiation [18–20]. However, under ultrafast cycling conditions, various problems are caused by unstable interfaces, such as between active materials and additives, electrodes and electrolytes, and electrodes and current collectors (CCs) [21]. Therefore, interface engineering is an important factor in facilitating the fabrication of ultrafast LICs.

Interfacial engineering is a key technique that improves cyclic stability and ultrafast capability via surface modification (such as by controlling the porous structure), nanostructuring, and functionalization [22]. In particular, it can enhance ion and electron transport, electrical conductivity, and structural and cycling stabilities [22]. Recently, most studies on interfacial engineering have focused on the interface between the electrode and electrolyte to enhance the cycling performance of the electrode. Although the interface between the electrode and CC significantly affects the ultrafast cycling performance and stability, it has received little attention. In particular, the surface of the copper (Cu) foil is oxidized and corroded by the by-products of the electrolyte, causing a decline in the ultrafast capacity and stability, owing to the decrease in electrical conductivity and exfoliation of the electrode [22,23]. Therefore, to achieve ultrafast performance and stability of LICs, interfacial engineering between the electrode and CC must be further investigated.

Herein, the interfacial engineering of a tailored sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) co-doped C quantum dot (SNCQD) interfacial layer on a Cu foil (SNCQD/Cu) using an ultrasonic spray system for highly stable and ultrafast LICs, is proposed. Because S and N co-doping provides additional electrons in the C structure, the SNCQDs exhibit excellent electrical conductivity. In addition, owing to the high number of surface functional groups of SNCQDs, they can be more uniformly coated than other C-based materials, such as graphene and C nanotubes. Furthermore, in an ultrasonic spray system, ultrasonic vibrations in the spray nozzle can produce fine droplets, facilitating a highly uniform interfacial layer when compared with other coating methods, e.g. dip or spray coating. The proposed interfacial layer can effectively prevent the oxidation and corrosion of the CC. Owing to the synergistic effects of oxidation and corrosion prevention, the interfacial adhesion and electrical conductivity of SNCQD/Cu are improved, thereby ensuring the highly stable and ultrafast cycling performance of LICs.

SNCQDs were fabricated using the hydrothermal method. First, 0.4 g of sodium sulfide (Na2S), 0.9 g of urea (CH4N2O), and 2.673 g of citric acid (C6H8O7) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water by stirring for 3 h to obtain a precursor solution. Next, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal stainless-steel autoclave. The stainless-steel autoclave was heated to 180 °C for 6 h and was cooled down naturally. The prepared solution was centrifuged twice at 10000 rpm for 20 min. It was dialyzed using a 6–8 kDa molecular weight cutoff membrane for 24 h. The resulting solution was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 48 h to obtain the SNCQD powder. To develop the tailored SNCQD/Cu interfacial layer using an ultrasonic spray coating system, 1.5 wt% SNCQDs and 0.75 wt% polyvinylidene difluoride were dissolved in N-methyl-2-pyrrolinone (NMP). The feed rate of the suspension, frequency of the ultrasonic spray nozzle, spray time, temperature of the hot plate, and distance between the nozzle and hot plate were set at 0.01 mm/s, 130 kHz, 16 min, 120 °C, and 25 cm, respectively. In addition, to verify the S-doping effect, NCQD were fabricated using the same process (but without a sodium fluoride coating on the Cu foil). The pristine Cu foil without any treatment, Cu foil with an NCQD interfacial layer, and Cu foil with an SNCQDs interfacial layer are hereafter denoted as Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu, respectively. Furthermore, to optimize the effect of the thickness of the SNCQD interfacial layer, we controlled the spray time to 8 min and 24 min (denoted as SNCQD/Cu-8 and SNCQD/Cu-24, respectively).

The nanostructures and the elemental distribution of SNCQDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2, KBSI Gwangju Center) with energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) mapping. The absorbance of the SNCQDs was measured using ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy. The crystal structure, chemical-bonding structure, and surface functional groups of SNCQDs were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max-2500), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Multilab2000, X-ray source of Al Kα), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, NICOLET iS 50 American Thermo). The surface morphologies of the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu were analyzed using field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and the interfacial structure of the SNCQD/Cu was examined using cross-sectional TEM after focused ion beam (FIB) milling. The crystal structure and chemical bonding structure of the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu were analyzed by XRD and XPS.

To evaluate the electrochemical performance attributed to the interfacial layer, coin-type half-cells (CR2032) were fabricated. These comprised fabricated samples such as the CC of the anode, Li metal foil as the counter electrode, a porous polypropylene membrane as the separator, and 1.0 M LiPF6 in a blend of ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (1:1 vol%) as the electrolyte. The anodes were manufactured using a slurry composed of 80 wt% soft C, 10 wt% Ketjen black (EC600JD), and 10 wt% PVDF (Arkema) in the NMP solvent. The dispersed slurries were cast on Bare Cu, NCQDs/Cu, and SNCQDs/Cu. Then, they were dried in a 120 °C vacuum oven for 10 h. Furthermore, for the full-cell of LICs, the cathode was fabricated, composed of 80 wt% YP50F (Kuraray), 10 wt% PVDF, and 10 wt% of Ketjen black as the active material, binder, and conductive agent, respectively. The pre-lithiation of anode were performed via the Li metal stripping, which is the process of directly contacting the Li metal foil and anode using a few drops of the electrolyte and removing the Li metal foil from the anode after 6 h. Pre-lithiation and cell assembling were conducted in a high-purity argon atmosphere glove box while maintaining the water and oxygen gas contents below 10 ppm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of all half-cells was performed to assess the interfacial resistance and kinetics of the electrodes in the 105–10−2 Hz frequency range at an AC voltage of 0.005 V. To determine the electrochemical performance of all electrodes, the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests of half-cells were performed using a battery cycler (WonATech, WBCS 3000) in the voltage range of 0.05–3 V (vs. Li/Li+) in a constant temperature chamber (at 25 °C). The cycling stability was measured at a current density of 100 mA/g up to 100 cycles, with the rate performance investigated at current densities of 100, 300, 700, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000 mA/g for 10 cycles. The ultrafast cycling capabilities of all electrodes were measured in up to 500 cycles at a current density of 3000 mA/g. Furthermore, GCD tests were performed at capacity rates (C-rate) of 0.1C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, 3C, and 5C to evaluate the rate performance and C-rate of 5C for 3000 cycles to evaluate the ultrafast cycling performance of LIC full-cells (voltage range of 2.5–3.8 V and up to 3000 cycles). After cycling, the cross-sectional FE-SEM images of all electrodes and cross-sectional TEM images with EDS-mapping after FIB milling of the SNCQD/Cu electrode were examined to evaluate the interfacial stability under ultrafast cycling tests.

Fig. 1 shows schematics of the fabrication processes of (a) SNCQDs and (b) SNCQD/Cu. First, SNCQDs were fabricated via hydrothermal treatment, centrifugation, and dialysis. During the hydrothermal process, citric acid amide was formed using citric acid and urea, followed by the deamination, dehydration, and dehydrocondensation of the citric acid [24]. In addition, the N atoms in urea and S atoms in Na2S were doped into a honeycomb structure of the C lattice. Second, the SNCQDs powder was dispersed in NMP and coated on a Cu foil using an ultrasonic spray coating system. The ultrasonic vibrations in the spray nozzle create fine droplets, facilitating the formation of a uniform SNCQDs interfacial layer on the Cu foil [25]. From the strong metal and support interaction point of view, the SNCQD layer strongly adhered to the surface of Cu foil owing to the presence of many hydrogen-containing functional groups at the surface of SNCQDs [23,26,27]. The metal foil was hydrophilic thin native oxide, whereas the slurry with carbon materials was hydrophobic, thus causing insufficient adhesion. Therefore, after coating SNCQDs on Cu foil, the surface of Cu CC became hydrophobic, and the resistance between Cu foil and carbon-based slurry was reduced, improving the adhesion between the electrode and Cu CC [28–30]. The SNCQD interfacial layer not only effectively prevented the oxidation and corrosion of the CC but also increased the electrical conductivity of the electrode.

A TEM analysis was performed to identify the morphology and structure of the SNCQDs. Fig. 2(a, b) depicts the low- and high-resolution TEM images. Fig. 2(a), depicts the uniformly distributed quantum-scale C dots (in the size range of 2–7 nm) with an average size of 4.53 nm. Furthermore, in Fig. 2(b), the lattice fringe spacing of SNCQDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (101) plane of graphite [21,31]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2(c)) also revealed the crystal structure of the SNCQDs corresponding to the (101) plane of graphite [21,31]. Furthermore, as shown in TEM-EDS images of Fig. S1, the C, N, and F atoms well present in the SNCQDs. Based on the XRD curves shown in Fig. S2(a) and XPS spectra shown in Fig. S2(b–d), it was confirmed that the NCQDs and SNCQDs have the crystal structure of amorphous graphite of the (002) plane, and N and S atoms were present in SNCQDs. As is evident from Fig. S2(e) of FT-IR, the NCQDs and SNCQDs have abundant hydrogen-containing functional groups. In the UV–vis absorbance spectrum shown in Fig. 2(d), the SNCQD dispersion exhibits two specific absorption peaks at approximately 202 and 335 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of graphitic sp 2 domains containing the CC and CN bonds and the n-π* transition of the conjugated CO bond, respectively [21,31]. The graphitic and conjugated π structures of the SNCDQs can effectively distribute electron transfer channels, providing excellent electrical conductivity. The fluorescent characteristics of the SNCQDs were confirmed using a UV flashlight at a wavelength of 365 nm, as shown in the inset photographs of the SNCQD dispersion in Fig. 2(d). The SNCQD dispersion was transparent and dark under visible light; however, it was fluorescent bright blue under a UV flashlight. These results indicate that the S- and N-doped CQDs were successfully fabricated on a uniform quantum scale without aggregation, facilitating the application of SNCQDs in ultrasonic spray coating systems.

Fig. 3(a–c) depicts the FE-SEM images of Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu, respectively. In contrast to the Bare Cu that exhibits a granular surface morphology owing to carbonaceous species such as ad-impurities and native oxide, the NCQD/Cu and SNCQD/Cu exhibit a uniform and sleek surface morphology owing to the ultrasonic spray-coated CQDs interfacial layer. To ascertain the thickness of the interfacial layer, the cross-sectional TEM of the SNCQD/Cu was performed after FIB milling (see Fig. 3(d)). It was confirmed that the SNCQD interfacial layer was successfully formed using an ultrasonic spray coating system with a uniform thickness of approximately 345.8–352.2 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(d). Therefore, the uniform SNCQD interfacial layer on the Cu CC can enhance the structural and electrochemical properties during charge/discharge cycles because of oxidation and corrosion prevention in the Cu CC and the increased interfacial adhesion between the electrode and the CC.

The XRD and XPS analysis were used to confirm the crystal structure and chemical bonding states of the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu surfaces. Because of intensely thin thickness of CQD interfacial layer compared to Cu foil, all samples only showed the peaks of Cu (see Fig. S3). All XPS spectra obtained were calibrated using the binding energy of C1s (~284.5 eV). In the C1s XPS spectra of the NCQD/Cu and SNCQD/Cu, as shown in Fig. 4(a), five characteristic peaks were exhibited at approximately 283.7, 284.5, 286.0, 287.6, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to the CC, CC, C–N/C–O/C–S, CO, and O–C=O bonding states. The relative peak area ratio of C–N/C–O/C–S to C–C/C=C of SNCQD/Cu (0.38) was higher than that of NCQD/Cu (0.32) because of additional S-doping in the NCQDs [32]. The N1s XPS spectra of the NCQD/Cu and SNCQD/Cu showed three characteristic peaks at approximately 398.9, 400.3, and 401.9 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, whereas the characteristic peaks of the Bare Cu were absent (see Fig. 4(b)). Herein, pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N were located at the edge sites of graphite, providing one and two p-electrons via the aromatic system, respectively [33]. Furthermore, in Fig. 4(c), the S2p XPS spectrum of the SNCQD/Cu exhibited two characteristic peaks at approximately 162.9 and 163.7 eV, corresponding to C–S–C 2p 3/2 and C–S–C 2p 1/2, whereas the Bare Cu and NCQD/Cu did not exhibit characteristic peaks. Thiophenic-S, which indicates that the doped S atom is located between two C atoms, can provide additional electrons through spin-orbital coupling [32,34]. Therefore, the NCQDs interfacial layer with outstanding electrical conductivity enhances the charge transfer capability. Additional S doping further enhance the electrical conductivity.

To validate the electrochemical kinetics of the electrode connected to the interfacial layer, coin-type half-cells were manufactured using Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu as the anode CC and EIS measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the charge transfer resistance (Rct) (represented by the circular arc area at the high-frequency region) of the SNCQD/Cu electrode (278.3 Ω) was small when compared with those of the NCQD/Cu (432.9 Ω) and Bare Cu electrode (509.3 Ω). This result suggests that the CQD interfacial layer reduces the interfacial resistance of the electrode and CC. Furthermore, additional S-doping in the NCQDs released additional electrons, leading to the enhancement of electrical conductivity. Consequently, the SNCQD/Cu electrode displayed a lower Rct when compared with that of the NCQD/Cu electrode. Furthermore, to estimate the Li-ion diffusion dynamics, the frequency-dependent phase angle plots (Bode plots) were obtained and fitted, as shown in Fig. 5(b). In general, the low-frequency region corresponds to the capacitive behavior. It is related to Li-ion diffusion, indicating the ideal capacitive nature when the phase angle approaches −90° [35–37]. The SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited a comparatively higher phase angle than those of the NCQD/Cu and Bare Cu electrodes, signifying superior capacitive features. The medium-frequency region is associated with the capacitive-resistive transition, and the phase angle involves contributions from charge-transfer effects at the interface [38,39]. It was confirmed that the phase angle of the SNCQD/Cu electrode had a peak shift toward a high frequency and the lowest peak intensity, implying a lower charge transfer resistance and excellent response when compared with those of the NCQD/Cu and Bare Cu electrodes. Furthermore, the relaxation time constant (τ0) in the interfacial charge transfer frequency region (10–104 Hz) of all electrodes was calculated as follows [15]:
(1)
τ 0
= 1 /
f 0

The τ0 of SNCQD/Cu electrode (2.7 ms) was small when compared with that of the NCQD/Cu (9.4 ms) and Bare Cu (16.0 ms) electrodes. This indicates that a fast response is caused by the fast charge transport rate because of the SNCQD interfacial layer. In essence, the increased charge transfer kinetics of the SNCQD/Cu electrode enhanced its ultrafast Li-storage capability.

GCD tests were performed at a current density of 100 mA/g for up to 100 cycles using coin-type half-cells with the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu as CCs for the anode to evaluate their application to LICs. In the first cycle, all electrodes exhibited a similar Li-ion intercalation reaction beginning at 1.2 V. Specific plateaus were not observed, with the trend rather sloping down because of the amorphous crystal structure of the soft C (Fig. 6(a)). The initial specific capacities of the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes increased slightly to 213.1, 217.7, and 219.1 mAh/g, respectively. Accordingly, the cycling stabilities after 100 cycles at a current density of 100 mA/g positively increased to 95.8, 97.9, and 99.4 %, respectively. These promising results are attributed to the prevention of oxidation and corrosion of the Cu foil, along with the increased interfacial adhesion between the electrode and CC. Furthermore, Fig. 6(c) depicts the rate performance results for all the electrodes at assorted current densities, ranging from 100 to 3000 mA/g. Based on the increased current density data from 100 to 3000 mA/g, the SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited the highest specific capacity of 87.0 mAh/g when compared with those of the NCQD/Cu and Bare Cu electrodes (specific capacity of 60.7 and 42.2 mAh/g, respectively). To evaluate the effect of the thickness of the SNCQDs interfacial layer, the ultrafast cycling tests of half-cell of SNCQD/Cu-8 and SNCQD/Cu-24 electrodes applied as the CC were performed. As is evident from Fig. S4, the SNCQD/Cu-8 and SNCQD/Cu-24 electrodes exhibited lower ultrafast cycling performances with stability than that of the SNCQD/Cu electrode. This result is attributed to the insufficiently thick SNCQD interfacial layer of the SNCQD/Cu-8 and an excessive thick interfacial layer of the SNCQD/Cu-24 electrode. The excellent rate performance of SNCQD/Cu was attributed to the outstanding electrical conductivity and charge transport rate of the NCQD interfacial layer and additional S-doping. This process can be extended to ultrafast cycling conditions at a current density of 3000 mA/g for 500 cycles (Fig. 6(d)). Similarly, the SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited a superior specific capacity of 80.8 mAh/g, with a capacity retention of 90.1 % after 500 cycles at an ultrafast current density of 3000 mA/g when compared with those of the NCQD/Cu (specific capacity of 54.5 mAh/g and capacity retention of 77 %) and Bare Cu electrodes (specific capacity of 27.7 mAh/g and capacity retention of 57.2 %). The improved ultrafast electrochemical performance and stability of the SNCQD/Cu electrode contributed to the synergistic effect of the SNCQDs interfacial layer by enhancing interfacial adhesion, preventing corrosion and oxidation of the Cu foil, and increasing the electrical conductivity.

Based on these results, a coin-type full-cell of LICs was fabricated using the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu as the CCs of the anode, with activated C (YP50F) as the active cathode material. Fig. 7(c) depicts the GCD curves of the LIC full-cell with the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes obtained at C-rates of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 5C. Although all the electrodes exhibited similar GCD curves with discharge times (256.7 s, 250.8 s, and 264.6 s for Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes, respectively) at C-rate of 0.1C, the SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited the longest discharge time (19.3 s) when compared with those of other electrodes (6.3 s of Bare Cu electrode and 10.1 s of NCQD/Cu electrode) at a high C-rate of 5C. As is evident from the rate performance results depicted in Fig. 7(d), all electrodes exhibited comparable specific capacities (44.5 mAh/g, 45.4 mAh/g, and 47.4 mAh/g for the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu electrodes, respectively) at a low C-rate of 0.1C. However, at the high C-rate of 5C, the SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited the highest specific capacity of 17.9 F/g when compared with those of the Bare Cu (5.5 F/g) and NCQD/Cu (11.5 F/g). This rate performance was consistent with the results of the half-cell test depicted in Fig. 6, which shows the highest specific capacity of the SNCQD/Cu electrode. During the ultrafast cycling test, the SNCQD/Cu electrode showed the highest CE (see Fig. 7d). After 3000 cycles at 5C (see Fig. 7e), the SNCQD/Cu electrode exhibited excellent cycling stability of 84.7 % against 76.1 % for the NCQD/Cu and 42.0 % for the Bare Cu electrode. As is evident from the cross-sectional FE-SEM images of all electrodes after the ultrafast cycling test, as shown in Fig. 8(a–c), the SNCQD/Cu and NCQD/Cu electrodes exhibited a connected interfacial structure outside both the SNCQDs and the NCQD interfacial layers. The Bare Cu electrode demonstrated a divided interfacial structure between the electrode and CC. In the enlarged FE-SEM images of the interfacial structure of all electrodes (as shown in Fig. 8(d–f)), the NCQD/Cu and SNCQD/Cu electrodes exhibited a contact interfacial structure between the electrode and CC with the interfacial layer, whereas the Bare Cu electrode exhibited an exfoliated interfacial structure from the CC. Furthermore, as is evident from the cross-sectional TEM and EDS mapping images after FIB milling of SNCQD/Cu electrode after the ultrafast cycling test (see Fig. S5), the contacts between the electrode, SNCQD interfacial layer, and Cu CC were well maintained. As shown in Fig. S6 depicting the Cu 2p XPS spectra of the Bare Cu, NCQD/Cu, and SNCQD/Cu after the ultrafast cycling tests with the electrode and CQD interfacial layer removed, the NCQD/Cu and SNCQD/Cu exhibited a significantly small oxidized phase, implying that the CQD interfacial layer effectively prevented the oxidation of the Cu foil. Therefore, it was concluded that the SNCQDs interfacial layer enhanced the ultrafast cycling performance and stability of LICs owing to the following synergistic reasons: (a) the interfacial layer effectively protected the oxidation and corrosion of the CC and enhanced the adhesion between the electrode and CC, resulting in increased, ultrafast cycling stability and (b) S and N doping enhanced the electrical conductivity and reduced the interfacial resistance, resulting in excellent ultrafast cycling performance.

This study proposes the use of an SNCQD interfacial layer, deposited via hydrothermal and ultrasonic spray coating, on a Cu foil to enhance the stability and cycling performance of LICs. The SNCQDs were uniformly coated on the Cu foil via the fine droplets formed by the ultrasonic vibrations. In the half-cell, the SNCQD/Cu electrode demonstrated excellent ultrafast cycling performance, such as superior Li-ion capability (87.0 mAh/g at current a density of 3000 mA/g) and cycling stability (80.8 mAh/g with capacity retention of 90.1 % after 500 cycles at an ultrafast current density of 3000 mA/g). Furthermore, the SNCQD/Cu- based full-cell LICs exhibited excellent ultrafast cycling performance (capacity retention of 84.7 % at a C-rate of 10C after 3000 cycles). These results are attributed to the synergistic effects of the SNCQD interfacial layer on the Cu foil. First, the increased cycling stability was ascribed to the suppression of oxidation and corrosion, and the increase in the interfacial adhesion of the Cu foil. Second, the enhanced ultrafast specific capacity was ascribed to the highly conductive SNCQD interfacial layers. In conclusion, the use of an interfacial layer of SNCQDs is a promising interfacial engineering approach to developing highly stable and ultrafast LICs.
Ki-Wook Sung: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing.
Keun-Young Ko: Experiment, Investigation, Data curation.
Hyo-Jin Ahn: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea (2021M3D1A2047047) and the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) granted financial support from the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE), Republic of Korea (No. 20219410100120).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2023.108797.
